title: Home
text:
Welcome to my coursework website for ISSS608: Visual Analytics & Applications.
This website showcases the visualisations, analysis, and exercises that I have completed throughout the module. It serves as a portfolio of my learning journey in visual analytics using Quarto and R.

LinkedIn

title: Exam Data Visualisation
text:
3.1 Bar chart of gender
Now let’s start with the easiest plot.

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER)) + geom_bar() + labs( 
  title = "Number of Students by Gender", 
  x = "Gender", 
  y = "Count" )









3.1.1 What this does

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER)) says: use exam_data and put GENDER on the x-axis
geom_bar() automatically counts how many students are in each gender category
labs() adds a chart title and axis labels

title: Exam Data Visualisation
text:
7.1 Bar chart of race
Now use the same idea for RACE:

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = RACE, fill = RACE)) + geom_bar() + labs( 
  title = "Number of Students by Race", 
  x = "Race", 
  y = "Count" ) + 
  theme_minimal()








This shows how many students belong to each race category.

title: Exam Data Visualisation
text:
8.1 Histogram of Maths scores
Now let’s visualise a numerical variable.

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) + geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") + labs( 
  title = "Distribution of Maths Scores", 
  x = "Maths Score", 
  y = "Frequency" ) + 
  theme_minimal()









8.1.1 What this does

x = MATHS puts maths marks on the x-axis
geom_histogram() groups the scores into intervals
binwidth = 10 means the bars cover ranges like 0–10, 10–20, 20–30, and so on

This helps you see:

Where most students’ maths scores lie
Whether the distribution is spread out
Whether there are very low or very high scores

title: Exam Data Visualisation
text:
9.1 Boxplot of Maths by Gender
Now let’s compare marks across categories.

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER, y = MATHS, fill = GENDER)) + geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) + labs( 
  title = "Maths Scores by Gender", 
  x = "Gender", 
  y = "Maths Score" ) + 
  theme_minimal()









9.1.1 Why this is useful
A boxplot helps you compare:

Median score
Spread of scores
Possible outliers

So instead of just counting students, you are now comparing their performance.

title: Exam Data Visualisation
text:
10.1 English vs Maths
Now a relationship plot:

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths Scores",
    x = "English Score",
    y = "Maths Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









This helps answer:

Do students who score higher in English also tend to score higher in Maths?
Is there a visible positive trend?

title: Home
text:
Overview
This website contains:


Take-Home Exercise
A collection of take-home assignments, with each page documenting the visualisations, methods, and insights developed for the module.


In-Class Exercise
A collection of in-class exercise pages showing hands-on practice with data visualisation and analysis techniques.


About
A short page explaining the purpose of this website and the role it plays in documenting my coursework.

title: Pre-Module: Basic Visualisation using Exam Data dataset via Quarto
text:
This page gives an introduction to using Quarto to showcase different visualisation techniques.
library(readr) 
library(ggplot2) 
library(dplyr)
This does three things:

title: Pre-Module: Basic Visualisation using Exam Data dataset via Quarto
text:
Bar chart of gender
Now let’s start with the easiest plot.

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER)) + geom_bar() + labs( 
  title = "Number of Students by Gender", 
  x = "Gender", 
  y = "Count" )









What this does

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER)) says: use exam_data and put GENDER on the x-axis
geom_bar() automatically counts how many students are in each gender category
labs() adds a chart title and axis labels

  x = "Race",

  theme_minimal()








This shows how many students belong to each race category.

English vs Maths

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH, y = MATHS)) +

title: Take-Home Exercises
text:
This section contains the take-home exercises completed for the module.





Exercise 2

Latest published

This exercise focuses on interactive visual analysis, dashboard design, and exploratory data analysis. This exercise explores the fundamentals of visual analysis and bridges the gap between data and insights.




Exercise 1

Earlier exercise

This exercise focuses on introductory visualisation techniques, basic charts, and first data storytelling tasks.

title: About
text:
This website was created for ISSS608: Visual Analytics & Applications.
Its purpose is to document and present the visualisations, analysis, and coursework outputs that I have completed throughout the module. The site acts as a structured portfolio of my work, allowing different exercises and assignments to be organised into separate sections for easy navigation.
The website was built using:

Quarto for website generation
RStudio for development
R for data visualisation and analysis
GitHub for version control
Vercel for deployment

Through this website, I aim to present my coursework in a clear, organised, and accessible format.

title: Hands-On Exercises
text:
This section contains the take-home exercises completed for the module.





Exercise 1

Done on 15/04/2026

This exercise focuses on introduction of ggplot2 and exploring its various functions.

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
This exercise looks to explore
library(readr) 
library(ggplot2) 
library(dplyr)
This does three things:

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Bar chart of gender
Now let’s start with the easiest plot.

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER)) + geom_bar() + labs( 
  title = "Number of Students by Gender", 
  x = "Gender", 
  y = "Count" )









What this does

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = GENDER)) says: use exam_data and put GENDER on the x-axis
geom_bar() automatically counts how many students are in each gender category
labs() adds a chart title and axis labels

  theme_minimal()








This shows how many students belong to each race category.

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
English vs Maths
Now a relationship plot:

ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = ENGLISH, y = MATHS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_bar
Now let’s start with the easiest plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_dotplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)

Bin width defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data. Pick better value with
`binwidth`.









Note: y scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.
The following code below does 2 steps:

scale_y_continuous is used to turn off the y-axis, and
binwidth argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_histogram()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       

`stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.









It is to note that the default bin is 30.

Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()
The following code chunk does the following:

bins argument is used to change the number of bins to 20,
fill argument is used to shade the histogram with light blue color, and
color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars in black


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")  










Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()
The following code changes the fill of the histogram by using sub-group of `aesthetic()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_density()
This computes and plots the kernel density estimate, which is a smoothed version of the histogram.
It is a useflu alternative to the histogram for continuous data that comes from an underlying smooth distribution.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()








The next code plots 2 kernel density lines by using colour or fill arguments of aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_boxplot()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()            








Notches are used in boxplots to help visually assess whether medians of distributions differ. If it does not overlap, it means that the medians are different.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_violin

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom_point()
This is to create scatterplot.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Combinatiation of geom objects
The code below shows the plot of data points on the boxplot by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point().

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
stat_summary()
This adds mean values by using stat_summary() and overrides the default geom.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
geom()
This adds mean values by using geom_() and overrides the default stats.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
section: Adding best-fit curve on a scatterplot
text:
Adding best-fit curve on a scatterplot
The code below shows geom_smooth() being used to plot a best-fit curve in the scatterplot. The default method used is loess

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.


`geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'









The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
facet_wrap()
This code wraps a 1-D sequence of panels into 2-D. This is generally a better use of screen space than facet_grid because most displays are roughly rectangular.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
facet_grid()
This code forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_grid(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
Working with Coordinates

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
text:
coord_flip()
Flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: Hands-On Exercise 1
section: Changing the range of x & y-axis
text:
Changing the range of x & y-axis
As the scatterplot below is slightly misleading due to the axis range not being equal,

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'









the code below fixes both axis range from 0 to 100.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
